translations.translation:
Xiahou Shang (Boren)
夏侯尚 (伯仁)
Lived ADca.184–226
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]

byZL181
onAugust 24th, 2025

Xiahou Shang, styledBoren, was the son of a cousin of[Xiahou] Yuan.
He was intimate and friendly with theCivil Emperor.AWhen theGrand ProgenitorstabilizedJi province[冀州],[Xiahou] Shangwas madeArmy Major. [Xiahou Shang] led cavalry when he was following in expeditions and conquests. He later becameLiterary Fellow of the General of All Purposes.
TheBook of Weistates:[Xiahou] Shanghad wisdom and strategy in planning and preparing. TheCivil Emperorvalued him, and they were friends from when they were of humble status.
When the State ofWeiwas first established, he was promoted toAttending Gentleman at the Yellow Gates. When the Hu ofDai commandery[代郡] revolted, theMarquisofYanling[鄢陵侯],[Cao] Zhang, was sent to suppress them;[Xiahou] Shangparticipated in military matters for[Cao] Zhang. They stabilized the lands ofDai[代] and returned. When theGrand Progenitordied atLuoyang[洛陽],[Xiahou] Shang,bearing tally, transported the catalpa coffin1toYe[鄴]. Due to his past achievements, he was enfeoffed asDistrict Marquisof Pingling [平陵亭侯], conferred asCavalier Regular Attendant, and promoted toArmy Leader of the Center.•••
Catalpa coffins were usually reserved for monarchs.↩
After theCivil Emperorascended the throne steps, he changed fief toTownship Marquisof Pingling [平陵鄉侯] and was promoted toGeneral who Conquers the SouthandInspectorofJing province[荊州刺史], havingacting tallyto serve asCommanderof various military affairs in the south.[Xiahou] Shangpetitioned:
“Liu Beihas dispatched an army to be atShangyong[上庸]. As the mountain roads are treacherous and dangerous, they will not expect us. If we send surprise troops to sneak through and go beyond even what they have not expected, then we can overwhelm them singlehandedly.”
Thus, he ordered his armies to attackShangyong[上庸], pacifying three commanderies and nine counties. He was promoted to becomeGrand General who Conquers the South.
AlthoughSun Quanclaimed to be a vassal,[Xiahou] Shangmade preparations to attack and suppress him. Later,[Sun] Quanwas indeed duplicitous. In the third year of theera [222], when theimperial chariotgracedYuan[宛], he sent[Xiahou] Shangto lead various armies alongsideCao Zhento besiegeJiangling[江陵]. The general of[Sun] Quan,Zhuge Jin, and the army of[Xiahou] Shangfaced off against each other at theYangzi River[江].[Zhuge] Jinferried across to an islet in theYangzi River[江] and divided his navy within theYangzi River[江]. Overnight,[Xiahou] Shangoversaw many “oiled boats” and commanded over ten thousand infantry and cavalry. Going downstream, he covertly ferried across, and then he attacked the armies of[Zhuge] Jin, pressing them from both sides in theYangzi River[江] and burning their ships. Attacking from both water and land, he defeated him. The city had not fallen when there was a great plague and an edict bade[Xiahou] Shangto lead his troops back.
His fief was increased by six hundred households, merging with the previous to make one thousand and nine hundred households. He was givenacting ax tallyand promoted toGovernor.Jing provincewas ruined and desolate. Outside, there were savages and barbarians. It and the borderingWuwere separated by theHan River[漢水], and many of its former people lived in theJiangnan region.[Xiahou] Shangopened a road fromShangyong[上庸] that travelled west for over seven hundredli. The mountain people, savages, and barbarians all came to yield, and within five to six years, thousands of households surrendered and submitted.
In the fifth year [224], he shifted in fief toTownship Marquisof Changling [昌陵鄉侯].[Xiahou] Shanghad a beloved concubine that he doted upon who competed for favor with hiswife. Hiswifewas a daughter of the Cao clan, therefore theCivil Emperorsent people to strangle and kill the concubine.[Xiahou] Shangwas sorrowful, falling sick and feeling confused. He buried his concubine deeply, but he could not help but yearn to see her, and so he promptly dug her up to gaze upon her. When theCivil Emperorheard about this, he angrily said, “The disrespect towards[Xiahou] ShangbyDu Xiwas indeed reasonable!”2Because [Xiahou Shang] was a longstanding official, his favor did notdecline.•••
Du Xihad said thatXiahou Shangwas not a helpful friend and unworthy of preferential treatment.↩
In the sixth year [225], the illness of[Xiahou] Shangand he returned to the capital. TheEmperorpersonally graced him on numerous occasions, held his hands, and wept tears. When[Xiahou] Shangdied, he was given the posthumous title of theGrievedMarquis.BHis son,[Xiahou] Xuan, succeeded him. In addition, three hundred households were subtracted from the fief of[Xiahou] Shangto gift the son of the younger brother of[Xiahou] Shang,[Xiahou] Feng, peerage ofMarquis within the Passes.
TheBook of Weicontains an edict:
“From a young age,[Xiahou] Shangwas my companion. He was completely sincere and utterly faithful. Although it can be said that we have different surnames, we were like flesh and blood. When he was inside, he was as a stomach and a heart,3and when he was outside, he was as claws and teeth.4He was deeply perceptive in his intelligence and strategy, and the plans that he created were superior to those of others. It is unfortunate that he died early, but what can be done about fate? I shall posthumously award him the seal and ribbon of theGrand General who Conquers the Southand theMarquisof Changling [昌陵侯].”•••
This is a metaphor. “A stomach and a heart” [腹心] refers to an essential and trustworthy minister.↩
This is a metaphor. “Claws and teeth” [爪牙] refer to a talented and brave military general.↩
